mnemonics.title: T.R.Y. C.A.T.C.H. H.E.R.
mnemonics.content: T-Ry: Test Run Your code in try block | C-Atch: Catch the exception | C-Atch: And Handle The Error Here | H-ER: Handle Exception in catch block. Use when writing exception handling code.

mnemonics.title: Inherit To Report It
mnemonics.content: Inherit from std::exception | To get built-in functionality | Report error message | Using what() method | It makes your custom exception standard-compliant. Remember when creating custom exception classes.

mnemonics.title: Throw The Ball To Catch It
mnemonics.content: THROW = You throw the exception object | THE = Then Execute Handling | BALL = Block All Low-level risks | TO = Try | CATCH = Catch the error and handle it. Use when understanding flow of exception handling.

acronyms.usage: Use TRY block to wrap code that might throw an exception. Always use before CATCH.

acronyms.expansion: Catch Anything To Handle Critically Here
acronyms.usage: CATCH block handles the thrown exception. Place after TRY block. Catch specific exceptions before base class exceptions.
THROW keyword is used to raise/throw an exception. Can throw built-in types or custom objects.
Custom exception classes created by programmers for specific error conditions in their applications.

visualTips.visualization: Picture an upside-down tree or family tree. std::exception is the trunk at the top. Branches are std::runtime_error, std::logic_error. Your custom exceptions are smaller branches coming off these. Think of water flowing down - exceptions catch from most specific (leaves) to most general (trunk).

Imagine a factory conveyor belt (TRY block). If a defective product (exception) appears, it's thrown like a ball into the air. A catcher (CATCH block) stands nearby with different nets. The right net catches the right exception. Wrong net = exception escapes unhandled.
Picture yourself throwing a red ball (exception object) upward. The ball travels up and then falls down into a catching net below. If no net is there, the ball hits the ground (program termination).
Imagine std::exception as a message bottle. The what() method is like reading the note inside the bottle. Each derived class puts its own custom message in the bottle by overriding what().

analogies.analogy: Like a custom alarm system in a building. Standard alarms (built-in exceptions) detect fire/smoke. A user-defined exception is like programming your own alarm for a specific threat (like unauthorized entry) that the standard system doesn't cover.

Like a safety net under a tightrope walker. The TRY is the tightrope (risky code). If the walker falls (exception thrown), the safety net (CATCH) catches them and handles the situation gracefully instead of crashing to the ground.

Like sending a registered letter with specific content. The letter is your exception object with custom data. The postal system delivers it (throw). The recipient opens and reads it (catch) to understand what went wrong and how to respond.

Like building a custom-shaped box inside a standard shipping container. You get all the container's features (standard methods like what()) but can customize the shape (custom data members) for your specific needs.
